description: Sling types, rigging hardware, load calculations, crane signals, critical lifts, and inspection for riggers and iron workers

wire_rope_slings.construction: Typically 6x19 or 6x37 classification — number of strands x wires per strand

types.two_leg_bridle: Two legs from single hook to two points on load

hitches.vertical: 100% of rated capacity — load hangs straight down from sling
hitches.choker: 75% of rated capacity — sling wrapped around load and back through eye
hitches.basket: 200% of rated capacity at 90 degrees (both legs vertical). Reduces with sling angle.

rated_capacities_single_leg_vertical.note: These are APPROXIMATE. Always check manufacturer tag and rated capacity. Actual capacity varies by construction.

inspection_rejection_criteria: 6 randomly broken wires in one rope lay, or 3 broken wires in one strand in one lay
Kinking, crushing, birdcaging, or core protrusion
Heat damage (discoloration or visible damage from welding, torch, electrical contact)
Corrosion — pitting on wire surfaces
End fitting damage — cracked, bent, or worn eyes/sockets/thimbles
Missing or illegible tag
Any visible distortion or evidence of overload

materials.nylon: Most common. Good for general lifting. Stretches 8-10% — absorbs shock loads. NOT for acid or hot (over 180F).
materials.polyester: Low stretch (3%). Better chemical resistance than nylon. Not for alkaline solutions. Temp limit 180F.
materials.polypropylene: Chemical resistant, floats. Lower strength than nylon/polyester.

rated_capacities_vertical_hitch.note: 2-ply doubles capacity. Always check manufacturer tag.

inspection_rejection_criteria: Cuts, tears, or holes
Burns (from welding, grinding, friction)
Melted or fused fibers
Acid or chemical damage (discoloration, soft spots, brittleness)
Broken or worn stitching (red core yarn visible = replacement)
Knots (reduce capacity 50%)
Missing or illegible tag
UV degradation (powdery surface, faded color)
synthetic_web_slings.DO_NOT: Never use synthetic slings near welding/cutting. One spark can sever a web sling instantly.

chain_slings.grade: Grade 80 (orange/purple) or Grade 100 (blue) only for overhead lifting. NEVER use Grade 30 (proof coil) or Grade 43 (high-test) for overhead lifting.

rated_capacities_single_leg_vertical_gra.note: Grade 100 is approximately 25% higher capacity than Grade 80 at same size

chain_slings.advantages: Heat resistant (to 400F for Grade 80), can be shortened with grab hooks, long lasting, can use in sling angles
inspection_rejection_criteria: Stretch — any link stretched more than 5% of original length
Wear — links worn more than 10% of original diameter
Nicks, gouges, or cracks in links
Bent or twisted links
Heat damage (discoloration above 600F for Grade 80 — derate or remove)
Missing or illegible tag
Weld repairs (NEVER field weld chain sling components)

sling_angle_effects.explanation: As the sling angle from vertical increases, the tension in each sling leg increases dramatically. This is the most common cause of rigging failure.

tension_multiplier_per_leg.90_degrees_vertical: 1.000 x load per leg (sling straight up and down)
tension_multiplier_per_leg.30_degrees: 2.000 x load per leg — DOUBLE THE LOAD
tension_multiplier_per_leg.below_30_degrees: NEVER rig below 30 degrees — sling tension increases exponentially

sling_angle_effects.rule_of_thumb: The wider the spread, the higher the sling tension. Keep sling angles above 45 degrees whenever possible. NEVER below 30 degrees.
sling_angle_effects.formula: Tension per leg = Total Load / (Number of legs x Sine of sling angle from horizontal). Or use: Load / (N x cos(angle from vertical))

types.anchor_shackle_bow: Wider body — use when multiple slings connect to one point, or when load shifts direction

shackles.sizing: Rated by Working Load Limit (WLL). Safety factor is 5:1 (shackle will break at 5x WLL).
shackles.pin: Always use the shackle's own pin. Never substitute bolts or rebar. Pin must be fully seated and cotter-pinned (safety-wired for alloy shackles).
shackles.inspection: Check for deformation, wear on pin and body, legible markings, proper pin engagement, no corrosion pitting

turnbuckles.use: Adjusting length/tension of wire rope, guy wires, bracing, plumbing columns and vessels
turnbuckles.note: Not typically rated for overhead lifting unless specifically designed and rated for it. Use for alignment and bracing.
turnbuckles.safety: Safety-wire or cotter-pin open body turnbuckles to prevent accidental loosening from vibration

beam_clamps.use: Temporary attachment point on structural steel for rigging, fall protection, or hoisting

types.C_clamp_style: Clamps onto flange. Verify flange thickness within clamp range.
types.girder_clamp: Adjustable jaw with set screw. For attaching chain falls or come-alongs.
types.beam_trolley: Rolls on bottom flange — allows horizontal movement of hoist.

beam_clamps.CRITICAL: Verify WLL marked on clamp. Ensure clamp is fully seated and tightened. Load must be within rated angle. Never exceed WLL.
beam_clamps.inspection: Check jaw for wear, screw threads for damage, swivel for free rotation, markings legible

spreader_bars_and_lifting_beams.spreader_bar: Compression member between two sling legs — reduces sling angle, distributes pick points wider. Must be engineered and rated.
spreader_bars_and_lifting_beams.lifting_beam: Beam that attaches to crane hook at center — load hangs from multiple points below beam. Used for long/awkward loads.
requirements: Must have engineering design and rated WLL marked on beam
Inspect before each use — welds, connections, straightness
Proof tested to 125% of rated load
Store properly — do not allow damage from storage

come_alongs_chain_falls.come_along_lever_hoist: Manual lever-operated hoist. Used for pulling, tensioning, and short-distance lifts. Rated capacity marked on unit.
come_alongs_chain_falls.chain_fall_chain_hoist: Chain-operated hoist. Faster operation than come-along for lifting. Hand chain operates through geared mechanism.
inspection: Check chain for damage, wear, stretch, twist
Test brake — load should hold at any position
Check hooks for deformation and latch operation
Verify load chain feeds freely through mechanism
come_alongs_chain_falls.NEVER: Never use a come-along or chain fall as a permanent support. They are for temporary positioning only.

crane_hand_signals.note: Standard hand signals per ASME B30.5 and OSHA 1926.1419-1422. Signal person must be qualified.

signals.HOIST_raise_load: Right arm extended, index finger pointing up, rotate hand in small circle
signals.LOWER: Right arm extended, index finger pointing down, rotate hand in small circle
signals.BOOM_UP: Right arm extended, thumb pointing up, fingers closed
signals.BOOM_DOWN: Right arm extended, thumb pointing down, fingers closed
signals.SWING_LEFT: Right arm extended, point in direction of swing with index finger
signals.SWING_RIGHT: Right arm extended, point in direction of swing with index finger
signals.STOP: Right arm extended palm down, move arm back and forth horizontally
signals.EMERGENCY_STOP: Both arms extended palm down, move arms back and forth horizontally
signals.TRAVEL_move_crane: Both fists in front of body, rotate around each other (direction of travel indicated by pointing)
signals.DOG_EVERYTHING_hold_position: Clasp hands together in front of body
signals.EXTEND_BOOM_telescoping: Both fists in front, thumbs pointing outward
signals.RETRACT_BOOM: Both fists in front, thumbs pointing toward each other
signals.RAISE_BOOM_AND_LOWER_LOAD: Right arm extended thumb up with fingers opening and closing
signals.LOWER_BOOM_AND_RAISE_LOAD: Right arm extended thumb down with fingers opening and closing

rules: Only ONE designated signal person at a time
Signal person must be in clear view of operator at all times
ANYONE can give STOP or EMERGENCY STOP signal — operator must obey from anyone
If operator loses sight of signal person: STOP all operations
Use radios for blind picks — but hand signals are primary method
Signal person wears high-visibility vest/gloves for visibility

steps: 1. Determine load weight (certified weight from vendor, calculated weight, or weigh on scale)
2. Add rigging weight (slings, shackles, spreader bars, softeners, tag lines — typically 3-5% of load weight for planning)
3. Determine lift radius (center of crane rotation to center of gravity of load)
4. Look up crane capacity at that radius from load chart (specific to boom length and configuration)
5. Compare: Load + rigging weight must be LESS than crane capacity at that radius
6. Apply safety margin — most sites require crane to be at less than 75% of chart capacity for routine lifts. Over 75% = critical lift requiring written plan.

pipe_weight_reference_lbs_per_foot.note: These are EMPTY steel pipe weights. Add water weight for hydro test: water = 8.34 lbs/gallon. Use pipe volume tables to calculate.

structural_steel_weight_reference.note: W-shape designation: W(depth in inches)x(weight in lbs per ft). Always verify from steel mill cert.

water_weight_in_pipe_lbs_per_foot.note: CRITICAL for hydro test lifts — water-filled pipe weighs 30-60% more than empty pipe depending on size/schedule

critical_lift_planning.definition: A lift requiring extra planning and approval. Triggers vary by site but commonly include:
triggers: Load exceeds 75% of crane rated capacity at operating radius
Lifts over occupied buildings or active process areas
Lifts near power lines (less than 20 ft clearance before lift begins)
Lifts in confined or congested areas
Lifts in wind above specified limits
Any lift the competent person determines requires extra planning
plan_contents: Load weight calculation with COG (center of gravity) location
Rigging plan with sling/hardware selection and calculations
Crane selection with load chart showing adequate capacity at all radii during lift

frequency.quarterly: Documented inspection with color-coded tag/tape (colors change quarterly: typically Jan=green, Apr=blue, Jul=yellow, Oct=red — varies by site)

rigging_inspection_program.color_code_system: All rigging equipment receives color-coded tag or tape at quarterly inspection. If color doesn't match current quarter, DO NOT USE — it hasn't been inspected.
storage: Hang slings on racks — never on the ground where vehicles can run over them
Protect synthetic slings from UV, chemicals, and heat
Keep all rigging clean and dry
